speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying, Henry, you were at another place, and there were young children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were at this little home for small children in Limoges. In Vichy, France, unoccupied France. And because all the children there were real small, the people that were running this place thought we'd be happier in a place where there were children of our age, of course. And the organization-- the [? OSE ?] I referred to earlier, had several homes not far from Limoges. And we were sent to a place called Le Chateau de Chabannes about 100 miles from Limoges-- 100 miles northeast, but still within Vichy, France, unoccupied France. We arrived there, and there were about 100 children there. Half of them were German or Austrian refugee children. The other half were French Jewish children. It was a nice place. It was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very countrified, in the middle of nowhere, in a very rural, backward part of France. Way off the beaten tourist paths. Even today, nobody ever goes there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were in this little village. And basically, we lived off-- off the land. We would go out and go to the local farmers, and try to buy vegetables, and fruit, and whatever meat we could, or chickens, or what have you. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] try to keep ourselves supplied as much as we could. Where the money came from at that point, we didn't know. And we didn't care, either. We were just glad to have a place to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the place--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, we didn't care-- it wasn't being callous. But we just weren't inquisitive. And we had learned, you know, that asking too many questions cannot-- is sometimes very unhealthy during wartime, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the place like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The place was very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nice, because we were finally among children our own age. We had to depend on one another to survive. Everyone had a job to do, and we did it. We became very good friends. And jumping the gun a little bit in my story, I have remained friends with most of the people, the other kids that I met there, until this very day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my brother, yes. There were, as I said, about 100 children divided into three groups. The little ones, which ranged up in age to about eight years old. The-- what we call the middle ones were about eight to 12. And the older ones, of which we were part, from 12 to, I think the oldest one was 16 or 16 and a half. I was still 14 at the time. We [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were going to the local school in that local village. It was what in the states they call a one-room schoolhouse, except there were two rooms-- one for the boys, and one for the girls. The French were very strict about that. And fortunately, we had two lady teachers, who were French ladies, French teachers, but were themselves refugees from Paris, having wanted to escape the Germans. And the local French kids from the village didn't like all these smart refugee kids coming into the school. And we did make them look bad a little bit, I must admit. And they started in with some anti-Semitic remarks and what have you. Because a lot of this spilled over from the occupied part of France. We were not too far from the border. They heard this on the radio. And in the occupied part of France, where the Germans were, they had now started their campaign against the French Jews. But these two schoolteachers would have none of it. They absolutely marched in there, and they would hit the kids. And just, you know-- this was legal. In the French schools, they were still, in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was everybody Jewish that was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was everybody Jewish that was there? And where these-- the teachers Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the home, everybody was Jewish. The teachers in the public school, in that little schoolhouse were not Jewish, no. No, no. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they would not buy any of this anti-Semitism. In fact, after we were there a while, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Vichy government, when a man named Pierre Laval became prime minister, he was a rabid anti-Semite-- wanted to pass a law and did pass a law that Jewish children could not go to French school-- these two schoolteachers simply said, this is not a law that we-- we don't discriminate against children. And they were both arrested by the French police. But had sufficient connections in the Ministry of Education or somewhere that they let them go. But they stood up for us Jewish kids. Wonderful ladies. And we're still in contact with them. We did that. We went to school in the morning. And in the afternoon, we went to a trade class. The [? OSE ?] was working with the French chapter of ORT-- 'ort'-- which is a vocational training organization for Jewish children worldwide. And they were teaching us how to work leather and make leather products like wallets, and purses, and what have you. So that when we got older and the war would be over some day, we would have a trade or something to fall back on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of the teachers? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: The names of the teachers? Oh, there were so many. They were counselors more than teachers. The teachers in the public school-- is that?

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- you mentioned two women that were--

speaker: interviewee
text: The two ladies?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. They were sisters, actually. And their family name is Payassous, which is a French name. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw them last in 1988. We had a reunion, and I'll tell you about that a little bit later on. They're retired of course, now, but still very much active.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the place like that you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: The place where we lived?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the-- was it a house, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was an old country house, Georgian style. You might call it a small mansion. There was a main house. There was an annex, which had formerly been used as stables and was now converted into dormitories. We had very little running water, and the sanitary facilities weren't always what they should be.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you're 14 or 15 years old, and you have to wash under a hand pump out in the yard, and it's 35 degrees out, so what? You know? It was fun, because we all did it together. But it was-- it was a little primitive.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had a roof over our heads. We had food. It wasn't good food. We-- the farmers would sell us things at very inflated prices. We ate certain vegetables, if you can call them that, that they used to feed-- feed to their pigs. But no humans would eat them. But our chef in the kitchen knew how to cook them and make things out of them. And we ate them for days. And it depended on the season of the year. The winters were tougher than the summers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there a year and three months, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left in 1941. I left in August 1941. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day, the director of the home called me into his office and said, I understand that you have relatives in the United States. And I said, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, well, did you have any papers? And I said, we did, but at one time when we were going to this French orphanage, we tried to pass ourselves off as French. And both my brother and I spoke fluently and without accent.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we took everything that was in any way related to Germany-- our passports, and any identity papers, and anything that made us, by trappings, Jewish, and we buried it in the yard in Orsay. And I don't have any papers. I don't have anything. I only have my-- my name. And you can check with the consulate, I guess, in Paris. He said, well, we can't go to Paris, but we can go to Marseille. There's an American consulate in Marseille, and we can find out if they know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he said to me, if you had a choice to make, would you go and try to find your parents in China, or would you rather go to the United States? And I said, of course, I'd rather go to the United States. Because I had no idea if my parents were alive or not. So he said, OK. What were their names? The directors? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Felix [? Chevrier. ?] He was a Frenchman. He was a former congressman in the French Parliament. He had also been a refugee from Paris. He was not Jewish. But he worked as director of this [? OSE. ?] And it was important that the head of these places be not Jewish and be someone connected with government, because it made the local village people more compliant and more cooperative, and they wouldn't-- But by the time 1941 came around, the French police were already in Chabannes, and they were questioning everyone. And they had been sent by Vichy. And of course, the Germans had put pressure on Vichy to try and round up the Jews in that unoccupied part of France. And Vichy cooperated fully. Fully. The French police were more thorough than the Germans and the Gestapo in that area. The Germans came and visited once. They just wanted to see who was there. Some German officers came. They really had no jurisdiction in that unoccupied. Part of France. But they didn't care. They came anyway, and they just nosed around a little bit. And then they left again. Then the next day, the gendarmes came, and they were more thorough. So we knew that it was a matter of time before they-- and eventually, after I had left, they rounded up a number of comrades and sent them. They wound up in Auschwitz and in some of those camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So after this interview with the director, a few weeks passed. And then he called us in, and he said, OK, you said to me that you wanted to go to the United States rather than to go to China. And I said, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, well, how about your brother? And I said, well, my brother feels the same way. And he said OK, one of you can go to the United States. We don't have enough money to send you both. So I said, well, then, let my brother go. And my brother and three other kids were sent to Marseilles and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eventually smuggled into Spain, then went to Portugal and came to the United States. And I'll tell you the details in a minute, because I followed that route a few months later.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the time came, I was called into the director's office. And he said, OK, it's your turn. [PHONE RINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I thought it turned that off. And so-- [PHONE RINGING] When was that, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in September 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry-- I'd like to correct that. It was in August-- the beginning of August, 1941. I left Chagannes on August 4th, 1941. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We were sent by train to Marseilles to a collection point where we met other kids that came from other homes of the [? OSE ?] also destined to go to the United States. And we found out that we had one thing in common, and that was that we all had relatives in the United States. And we spent about three or four weeks in Marseilles waiting for the right moment or opportunity-- we didn't know what-- for these people to take us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out of France. We didn't know how we were going to get out of France, but they told us it can be at any time. And sure enough, there was one night when actually, there was an air raid. The British were bombing Marseilles very heavily. And a truck came, and we were woken up in the middle of the night and asked to take our things and go in the truck. They took us to the train station, and the train took us to the Spanish border. And there was a-- a man that met us there. And we walked for a short distance. It wasn't very long, about two hours. Through some hills and mountains and-- no real mountain climbing. It was just hilly country. And he said, well, you're in Spain now. And he took us to another railroad station. There was somebody there waiting for us who was an American. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said to us that he was from the Joint Distribution Committee in the United States, and he would look after us. But we were illegal in Spain. Because Franco-- Spain was siding with the Nazis also in World War II, so we had to be very careful.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in these homes, did you have any type of religious observances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We tried to observe the Jewish holidays. We conducted our own services. Some of us had been educated and gone through Hebrew school, myself included. I was reading liturgical Hebrew fluently. I don't speak the language as it's spoken in Israel, but I know liturgical Hebrew. And I-- through the routine of having gone to services Fridays and Saturdays when I was a kid at home, I knew how to conduct a service. There were one or two other kids that were also knowledgeable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a service. Those who wanted to attended did, and those who didn't, didn't. But we did observe that. We knew-- we knew when it was Passover. We didn't have the wherewithal-- the matzo and all things like that. But we knew it was Passover, and the people in the kitchen tried to do something a little bit extra if they could. And we would tell the story of the Exodus in French. And because we had no books or anything, you know? So we did-- yeah. We were always conscious that we were Jews and our counselors, they would teach us songs in Hebrew. And we would do some of the Israeli folk dances and things like that, yes. We always had that spirit. Now, you said-- so you went to Spain. And when exactly was this, and how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this was-- at the end of September 1941. And we arrived by train in Madrid and this gentleman took us on a little bus that was waiting. And we went to a convent, and there were these Spanish nuns. And we stayed at this convent for a few days, because, as I said, we were illegal in Spain. We had no papers. And the Spanish authorities with us would have turned us over to the Germans and the Vichy French.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- by train. This man that had met us at the border from the Joint Distribution Committee had train tickets for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many kids were with you? About a dozen. We were about a dozen all together. I was the oldest in that particular group.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, as I said, we stayed for about three days in Madrid. And the nuns-- well, [LAUGHS] they sort of cleaned us up, literally and figuratively. And they gave us some good food for a change. They gave us some new clothing. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they got rid of the fleas and the other little creatures and things that, you know, we had acquired in Marseilles, because conditions there weren't all that great. But anyway, we got out of there, and we were sort of like new human beings. And then they-- the man had train tickets to the Spanish border for us. And at the Spanish border, we were met by an American couple, [INAUDIBLE] and his wife, who worked for the Joint Distribution Committee. And they were supposed to be our escorts for the United States. And we got onto a train that took us from the Spanish border to Lisbon. And we went to a little college outside of Lisbon, a boarding school. And we waited there about three weeks, because apparently, it was very difficult to get space on board a ship. Only the neutral ships could cross the Atlantic with civilians on board. And there weren't many neutral countries. There was Switzerland, but they had no Navy. [LAUGHS] They had no ships. And that left Portugal or Sweden. And we were-- we were too far away from Sweden. So the Portuguese ships were all booked in advance for months and months and months. So we didn't know which ship we would get on and when. But after about, oh, I think it was about, again, three weeks or so, we were told that we would leave the next day. And it was a Portuguese ship called the And the was like a big shuttle between Lisbon and New York. There are literally thousands of refugees in the United States that came here on that ship. And it was, at that time, one of the largest passenger ships afloat. By today's standards, it's very small. But now, we had-- the ship had-- was supposed to go in eight days from Lisbon to New York. That was the standard time in those days. And they had enough provisions for eight or nine days on board for all the people who-- the refugee kids went in steerage, which was way down below the water level. And that were-- we slept in bunks four high. On sort of a straw mattress, but we didn't care. We were just glad to get out of there. And since food and things like that were hard to buy in Europe-- every country had shortages, even the neutral ones-- the ships were usually replenished in the United States. So they had enough food on board for eight days. And we were about two days out of Lisbon when the boat came to a stop one morning. And that was very unusual. And we ran up, got dressed, and run-- ran up on the deck. And there was a German submarine that had stopped us. And although we were neutral, they had said, 'well, you're neutral--' we found this out later when the crew explained it-- 'you have enemies of the Third Reich on board, and we have to control your passenger list.' And they directed us to go to the nearest port, which happened to be Casablanca in Morocco. And Casablanca, Morocco, was under the Vichy government. It was still a French colony, and it was under the Vichy government rules. The political situation at that point in France was very confusing. You had the Vichy government, you had the free French under the Gaulle operating out of London, and in North Africa, you had another free French group, which was, however, collaborating with Vichy under a general called [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened--?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were all fighting each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in Morocco?

speaker: interviewee
text: So in Morocco, some Germans and Vichy French gendarmes came on board, and they wanted to see our papers. And all I had was a French residence permit, which I had received when I got to Chabannes, and I had to go to the police there every month to have it stamped so I could stay another month. And I showed them that, and they wanted to know, did I have any photographs that would show any buildings, or bridges, or, you know, things of that sort. And I said no, I have no photographs. I don't have anything. Because all I literally had with me was the clothes on my back and a little backpack that had a mess kit in it. And that was about it. And, I think, a change of underwear. Other than that, there was-- [LAUGHS] we had no luggage and no belongings. So we stayed in Casablanca about three days. And then they let us go. And this is important, because it added three days to our itinerary. And we got out of Casablanca and were heading for New York. And we got another four or five days, when one very early morning at dawn, we heard gunfire from a cannon. Again, we ran on deck. And there was this warship flying a British flag. And it was a British destroyer. And it was, again, the same story. 'Yes, you're a neutral ship, and we mean you no harm, but you have Germans on board.' We-- [LAUGHS] we were the threat to the Allies! Poor kids! 'And we have to escort you to the nearest Allied port,' which happened to be Hamilton Bermuda.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the poor limped into Bermuda. And by this time, we were 15 days out of Lisbon, and we had no food, or water, or anything. You know, it was-- the last three days there was really rough. In Bermuda, they-- they gave us some food and things. And the British went through the same routine as the Germans and Vichy French did. They wanted to know, where do you come from? Where are you going? What did you do in France? Where were you? Do you have pictures? Do you, you know-- they were looking for spies. So they let us go after about two days. So in all, the journey took about three weeks. And then we arrived in New York in October of 1941. Did you speak English?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke a little bit of English, because I had-- both in Germany, and in France in school, we were required to take a foreign language. And in Germany, having no idea that I would someday go to France, I had opted for English, because we were going to the United States, you know, quote, unquote. [LAUGHS] And I had studied some English. And little did I know that I would have to learn French.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: So in New York, when apparently the story of this voyage, which-- which was the last trip that the ship made before the US entered into the war-- the story of this little odyssey had made the news papers, or the news media in New York. And we were met outside of New York Harbor by a-- I guess it was a Coast Guard cutter or something full of news people. And they had film cameras. In those days, they had the movietone news, or whatever they call that. That was a newsreel-- newsreel. And there were some reporters that came on board, and they rounded up this bunch of kids, who had been ignored throughout the voyage by all the other passengers. And all of a sudden, we were the very important people that the press came to meet. [LAUGHS] And And they rounded us up, and they took pictures of us. And they wanted to know if somebody spoke English. And I said, well, I speak very little. And they said, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was the oldest, so they started to say, well, you know, are you OK? Yes. OK. Are you happy to be here? Yes. I just repeated the question with a pos-- [LAUGHS] positive, because-- And some of the questions I didn't understand, and others-- you know. And they said, well, was it very bad in-- in Europe? Yes, it was very bad. Did you see the war? Yes, we saw the war. Where-- where are your parents? And, you know, questions like that. It was in the newsreel. Did any of your family meet you there? Or who met you there? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ship docked in Staten Island, New York. And I had an uncle who had immigrated from Germany earlier, my mother's brother-- this is my mother's family, now-- who lived in the Bronx. And he and his wife met us at the ship. And we went live with them in the Bronx. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my uncle who lived there-- besides his wife, there was his little daughter. And there was also my grandmother and my grandfather that lived with them. So he already supported a large family. And now, my brother had arrived a month earlier than I did. And he was with them, and I was. And my uncle said, 'look--' by this time, I was 16, just barely 16. 'You boys have to go to work, because I can't afford this. I just don't make enough money to, you know, support eight people in this household.' [LAUGHS] It's just not-- So we said, sure, fine. OK, you know. But I-- I had always been a studious boy. And I-- I wanted more than anything else to go to school, to a real school, and get a real diploma. Because I had done my elementary school in Germany, I had done my secondary education in French in France. And I was all mixed up. I didn't know where I belonged. And now I was supposed to study English and go to school. So-- but the first thing was to get a job. So somehow through some friends, I don't know, I got a job to work in a bakery in the Bronx. And this little bakery was owned by a German Jewish couple who had been here for many years. They emigrated back in the 20s, but they were-- they spoke-- spoke German, so I could communicate with them. And I worked from 7:30 in the morning until 6 o'clock in the afternoon, six days a week, for $8 a week. That was the prevailing wage at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where were-- what happened to your parents and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1941, now. And I had been working for about a month when Pearl Harbor Day arrived. And the United States was at war with Japan. And now, of course, there was absolutely no hope of getting any news whatsoever from my parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My grandparents, who had been in the-- in New York, in the Bronx, and my uncle and aunt had had some news from my parents. They had received some letters. And while life was very tough for them, they had survived. But all communication stopped again on December 7th, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to school and--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to work. I went to work, as I said, during the day. And after work, I went to high school at night. Because I didn't know enough English to get into college. And I did a full high school program in two years. I graduated. I was valedictorian of my class. I went to evening high school in the Bronx and took a full academic course. The only advantage I had was that I didn't have to take courses in languages, because I spoke fluent German and fluent French. So I passed what they called the regents in those days, which were state-wide exams. And without any trouble. So that gave me a little leg up. But all the other subjects, I had to start from scratch.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop right here, and we're going to be-- going to the next tape, doing some closing questions in that. Thank you.
